hero.description: Autonomous agents need more than payments. They need {credit}, {programmableRules}, and {verifiableSettlement} — all without a central counterparty. Ergo is the only settlement layer built for this.

stripeProblemItems.title: No persistent identity
stripeProblemItems.body: Agents spin up and disappear. Stripe requires KYC, billing accounts, and static merchant IDs — none of which work for ephemeral autonomous processes.

stripeProblemItems.title: One-time payments only
stripeProblemItems.body: Agents need credit: issue now, redeem later. Stripe has no programmable IOU layer. Every micro-call requires a round-trip to a payment processor.

stripeProblemItems.title: No programmable acceptance
stripeProblemItems.body: You can't tell Stripe 'accept payment only if the agent completed task X and holds credential Y.' Logic lives outside the payment layer — fragile by design.

stripeProblemItems.title: Millisecond economics break
stripeProblemItems.body: A $0.001 API call through Stripe costs more in fees than the call itself. The rails weren't built for machine-to-machine micropayments at any scale.

whyErgoItems.title: eUTXO: verifiable atomicity
whyErgoItems.body: Every transaction is deterministic. Agents know exactly what will happen before submitting. No reentrancy, no hidden global state, no MEV surprises.

whyErgoItems.title: ErgoScript: programmable trust
whyErgoItems.body: Acceptance predicates are first-class primitives. 'Accept this note only if task hash matches and deadline hasn't passed' — one script, on-chain, trustless.

whyErgoItems.title: Babel Fees: agents don't need ERG
whyErgoItems.body: Agents can pay transaction fees with any token. No need to pre-fund an ERG wallet for every new agent instance — critical for ephemeral compute.

whyErgoItems.title: Sigma Protocols: private credit
whyErgoItems.body: Zero-knowledge proofs are native. Agents can prove they hold a credential or completed a task without revealing which agent they are.

whyErgoItems.title: Proof-of-Work: no chain halts
whyErgoItems.body: PoW has no governance emergency stops. Agent infrastructure built on Ergo won't be frozen by a validator cartel or foundation decision.

whyErgoItems.title: Native tokens: community money
whyErgoItems.body: Issue a community currency in one transaction. Notes, trackers, and reserve tokens are protocol-level — no custom ERC-20-style contracts required.

stack.heading: The agent economy stack
stack.description: Five layers are live on Ergo today. Two remain open research problems — the most interesting ones for builders to tackle.

stackLayerItems.label: Reserves
stackLayerItems.description: On-chain capital backing — holds ERG or tokens as collateral for issued notes

stackLayerItems.label: Trackers
stackLayerItems.description: Verifiable accounting — track balances, credit limits, redemption history

stackLayerItems.label: Notes
stackLayerItems.description: Programmable IOUs — issue, transfer, and redeem off-chain money

stackLayerItems.label: Trust Rules
stackLayerItems.description: Acceptance logic — 'accept this note if and only if...' — fully on-chain

stackLayerItems.label: Reputation
stackLayerItems.description: Agent reputation without a central oracle — active research area

stackLayerItems.label: Identity
stackLayerItems.description: Persistent agent identity across sessions without deanonymisation

useCases.heading: Three flows. All live on testnet.

useCaseItems.title: LLM buys inference
useCaseItems.flow: Agent → issues note → API provider accepts predicate → delivers tokens
useCaseItems.detail: One call, one proof, no persistent account. Provider sets their own acceptance rules — minimum task hash, maximum credit, deadline.

useCaseItems.title: Agent pays on credit
useCaseItems.flow: Reserve deployed → notes issued against it → tracker monitors usage → auto-settle
useCaseItems.detail: Credit limit enforced on-chain. No counterparty risk — the reserve contract is the bank.

useCaseItems.title: Community currency
useCaseItems.flow: Reserve holds ERG → community issues local notes → acceptance predicates define rules
useCaseItems.detail: A local marketplace, a compute cooperative, or an agent network — each with their own money and trust rules.

bml.heading: We're building the reference stack.
bml.description: Notes, credit, trackers, reserves, acceptance predicates — and the showcase for agent-to-agent payments. If you're building in this space, we want to talk to you.

finalCta.title: Building agents? Talk to us.
finalCta.description: Tell us what you're building. We'll reach out within 24 hours with a concrete next step — demo, design session, or code review.
